items.question_text: Complete: Whenever you become aware that an emergency _____ or will occur, take action to provide for the operation of all appropriate airport lighting aids as required.
items.explanation: The trigger covers both emergencies already in progress ('has' occurred) and those anticipated ('will' occur). A formal declaration is not required.

choices.text: has

items.question_text: A controller overhears a pilot report engine trouble but no emergency has been declared. The controller anticipates a possible emergency landing. Under 3-4-1, is action required?
items.explanation: The trigger is awareness that an emergency 'will occur' — formal declaration is not the threshold. The controller should act on the reasonable anticipation of an emergency, not wait for a formal declaration.

choices.text: No — action is required only after a formal emergency declaration or MAYDAY call.
No — the rule applies only to emergencies currently in progress, not anticipated ones.
Yes — the controller should provide for appropriate lighting aids because the trigger is awareness that an emergency will occur.
Yes — but only after coordinating with airport management for approval.

items.question_text: Complete: When separate on-off controls are provided, operate REIL when the associated runway lights are _____.
items.explanation: REIL are operated when the associated runway lights are 'lighted.' If the runway lights are off, REIL shouldn't normally be on.

choices.text: lighted

items.question_text: When should REIL be turned off after an arriving aircraft has landed?
items.explanation: REIL must be turned off after the aircraft has landed and cleared the runway, not while it's still occupying the runway or after it reaches the gate.

choices.text: Immediately upon the aircraft touching down.
After the aircraft has taxied to the gate.
After the aircraft has landed and cleared the runway.
REIL stay on continuously as long as the runway is in use.

items.question_text: Under 3-4-3, VASI systems with remote on-off switching must be operated when they serve the runway _____.
items.explanation: VASI must be operated when they serve the 'runway in use' and intensities are controlled per the applicable table.

choices.text: in use

items.question_text: PAPI systems with remote on-off switching must be operated when they serve the runway in use and intensities are controlled in accordance with which table?
items.explanation: PAPI intensity settings are controlled per TBL 3-4-4. VASI uses TBL 3-4-2 and TBL 3-4-3, but PAPI has its own dedicated table.

items.question_text: Under 3-4-5, approach lights must be operated between sunset and sunrise when they serve the _____ runway.
items.explanation: The first condition is that they serve the 'landing' runway. They are also operated when serving a runway with an approach being made.

choices.text: landing

items.question_text: What table controls ALS intensity settings under 3-4-6?
items.explanation: ALS intensity settings are controlled per TBL 3-4-5.

items.question_text: Complete: SFL are a component of the ALS and _____ be operated when the ALS is off.
items.explanation: The note says SFL 'cannot' be operated when the ALS is off. They are a subsystem, not an independent lighting system.

choices.text: cannot

items.question_text: Under 3-4-7, when must SFL be operated?
items.explanation: SFL must be operated when the visibility is less than 3 miles AND instrument approaches are being conducted to the runway. Both conditions must be met — it's a conjunction, not either/or.

choices.text: Any time visibility is less than 3 miles, regardless of whether approaches are in progress.
Only when the visibility drops below 1 mile.
When visibility is less than 3 miles and instrument approaches are being conducted to the runway.
Only between sunset and sunrise.

items.question_text: MALSR/ODALS with separate on-off and intensity setting controls are operated in accordance with which tables?
items.explanation: The paragraph references both TBL 3-4-6 and TBL 3-4-7 for MALSR/ODALS control.

choices.text: TBL 3-4-6 and TBL 3-4-7

items.question_text: Complete: When the prevailing visibility is _____ mile or less or the RVR is 4,000 feet or less, operate the ALSF-2 system as requested by the pilot or as deemed necessary.
items.explanation: The ALSF-2 operation threshold is visibility of 3/4 mile or less or RVR 4,000 feet or less.

items.question_text: Under 3-4-10, runway edge lights serving the runway in use must be operated between sunset and sunrise and at what other times?
items.explanation: Runway edge lights are also operated between sunrise and sunset when the ceiling or visibility is below basic VFR minima. This daylight extension applies whenever weather degrades below VFR thresholds.

choices.text: Only between sunset and sunrise — never during daylight hours.
Whenever any aircraft is on the runway, regardless of time or weather.
Between sunrise and sunset when ceiling or visibility is below basic VFR minima.
Only when specifically requested by a pilot.

items.question_text: High intensity runway and associated centerline and touchdown zone lights are operated in accordance with which table?
items.explanation: The controlling table is TBL 3-4-8, with exceptions for facility directives, pilot requests, and controller judgment.

items.question_text: HIRL that control the associated MALSR are operated in accordance with which table?
items.explanation: HIRL associated with MALSR use TBL 3-4-9, with exceptions for pilot requests and controller judgment.

items.question_text: Complete: Keep the appropriate approach controller or PAR controller informed, in advance if possible, of _____ changes that affect RVR.
items.explanation: HIRL changes that affect RVR must be communicated to approach or PAR controllers in advance if possible.

choices.text: HIRL

items.question_text: MIRL or MIRL which control associated MALSR are operated in accordance with which table?
items.explanation: MIRL uses TBL 3-4-10.

items.question_text: Under 3-4-15, when must high speed turnoff lights be operated?
items.explanation: High speed turnoff lights are operated whenever the associated runway lights are used for arriving aircraft. They stay on until the aircraft enters a taxiway or passes the last turnoff — not just at night or on request.

choices.text: Only during nighttime operations when visibility is below 3 miles.
Only when specifically requested by an arriving pilot.
Whenever the associated runway lights are used for arriving aircraft, until the aircraft enters a taxiway or passes the last turnoff.
Only for departures, because turnoff lights guide aircraft exiting onto taxiways.

items.question_text: Taxiway lights are operated in accordance with which tables under 3-4-16?
items.explanation: Three alternative tables control taxiway lights: TBL 3-4-11, TBL 3-4-12, or TBL 3-4-13.

items.question_text: Complete: If controls are provided, turn obstruction lights on between _____ and sunrise.
items.explanation: Obstruction lights are operated between 'sunset' and sunrise when controls are provided.

choices.text: sunset

items.question_text: Under 3-4-18, the rotating beacon must be turned on between sunrise and sunset when reported ceiling or visibility is below which threshold?
items.explanation: Between sunrise and sunset, the rotating beacon is operated when ceiling or visibility is below basic VFR minima. Between sunset and sunrise, it operates regardless of conditions.

choices.text: Below CAT I ILS minimums.
Below basic VFR minima.
Below special VFR minima.
Below 1,000 feet ceiling or 3 miles visibility.

items.question_text: What are Runway Status Lights (RWSL) designed to do?
items.explanation: RWSL are a fully automated system that provides direct visual alerts to pilots when it is unsafe to enter, cross, or takeoff from a runway. Controllers do not directly operate them — the system acts autonomously based on surveillance data.

choices.text: Serve as backup lighting when the primary runway lighting system fails.
Replace runway edge lights with a higher-intensity system for all operations.
Provide automated visual alerts to pilots indicating runway occupancy and approach conflicts.
Provide sequenced flashing guidance for departing aircraft only.
